strengths: High accuracy using state‑of‑the‑art hosted LLM
Fast to implement with existing vector DB services
Scales well with managed vector store and API rate limits
weaknesses: User data leaves the premises when calling LLM API
LLM can hallucinate citations despite retrieval grounding
architecture_scores.document_update_handling: Re‑embed only changed chunks; vector DB supports incremental updates.

strengths: All data stays on‑premise, maximal privacy
Potential to embed citation patterns directly in model
weaknesses: Fine‑tuning large models requires GPU time and expertise
Longer development cycle; re‑training needed for each document set change
Open‑source LLMs typically lag behind hosted APIs in raw accuracy
architecture_scores.document_update_handling: Any substantial change forces a new fine‑tune run, making frequent updates costly.

strengths: Very low cost, no external API calls
Exact keyword matches give perfect citation traceability
Simple to maintain when documents change
weaknesses: Poor natural‑language understanding; low answer quality
Cannot handle paraphrased questions or reasoning
architecture_scores.document_update_handling: Index rebuild is trivial; just re‑run keyword extractor.

strengths: Best possible accuracy through multi‑step retrieval, query rewriting and citation verification
Citation reliability is highest because verification step checks source presence
weaknesses: High operational complexity; many moving parts (rewriter, ranker, verifier, LLM)
Longer development time, higher compute cost
More components to secure and monitor
architecture_scores.document_update_handling: Requires re‑indexing and may need re‑tuning of ranker; still manageable but adds coordination overhead.

implementation_rationale: Option A delivers the strongest combination of accuracy, rapid delivery, and manageable cost while staying within the 6‑week MVP window. It meets the core requirement of providing answer citations and can be built using off‑the‑shelf vector‑DB services and a hosted LLM API. Privacy concerns are mitigated by encrypting data at rest, limiting the payload sent to the LLM (only the retrieved chunks), and applying strict data‑retention policies. The architecture is extensible: later phases can introduce query‑rewriting, reranking, and verification (Option D) to boost accuracy and citation reliability without re‑architecting the whole system.
risks: Confidential document content is transmitted to an external LLM provider.
LLM may hallucinate or omit citations, reducing trust.
Per‑token API costs could grow as user base scales to 20k users.
Vector‑DB latency or quota limits may affect response time under load.
Frequent document updates require re‑embedding, which could become a bottleneck if not automated.
mitigations: Encrypt PDFs before upload; decrypt only in memory for embedding.
Send only the minimal retrieved text chunks (e.g., top‑3) to the LLM, never the full document.
Implement a prompt template that forces the model to cite source IDs and validate that the cited IDs appear in the retrieved set.
Negotiate volume pricing or switch to a self‑hosted open‑source embedding model to control embedding costs.
Automate the ingestion pipeline: on document upload, trigger chunking, embedding, and upsert into the vector store.
Monitor API usage and set budget alerts; introduce caching for repeated queries.
Plan a phased upgrade to Option D after MVP, reusing the existing vector store and adding a lightweight reranker.
mvp_plan: Week 1: Set up secure file upload service, PDF parsing, chunking (≈200‑300 token chunks), and choose an open‑source embedding model (e.g., Sentence‑Transformers) to run in‑house.
Week 2: Deploy a managed vector database (e.g., Pinecone, Qdrant, or self‑hosted Milvus). Build ingestion pipeline that embeds chunks and stores them with document and chunk IDs.
Week 3: Integrate hosted LLM API (e.g., OpenAI gpt‑4o) with a prompt that includes: retrieved chunks, user question, and a request to return citations in the format "[docID:chunkID]".
Week 4: Develop front‑end UI for document upload, question entry, and answer display with clickable citations that open the source PDF at the relevant page.
Week 5: Harden security – TLS everywhere, at‑rest encryption, role‑based access, data‑retention policy (delete raw PDFs after embedding), and implement logging/audit.
Week 6: Load‑test for 500 concurrent users, fix any latency issues, finalize monitoring (LLM usage, vector DB latency), and launch MVP.
Post‑MVP (Month 2‑4): Add query‑rewriting and reranking layer, implement citation verification step, and evaluate cost‑effective self‑hosted LLM inference to reduce API spend.
